seo.title: Clerk vs Auth.js vs Supabase Auth
seo.description: A practical comparison of Clerk, Auth.js, and Supabase Auth for Authentication & Authorization.

content.intro: Comparing authentication solutions for developers balancing implementation speed, customization, and long-term maintainability. Focus on trade-offs between hosted services, open-source frameworks, and self-hosted options.

options.name: Clerk
options.tagline: Hosted authentication with built-in UI components
options.best_for: Startup teams needing rapid authentication implementation with minimal infrastructure

options.name: Auth.js
options.tagline: Open-source authentication library for Next.js and Node.js
options.best_for: Full-stack developers requiring custom authentication flows with JWT and OAuth support

options.name: Supabase Auth
options.best_for: Teams building data-driven applications requiring database-coupled authentication

criteria.name: Implementation Effort
criteria.description: Time required to integrate authentication into application

criteria.name: Lock-in Risk
criteria.description: Difficulty of switching to alternative authentication systems

criteria.name: Reliability
criteria.description: Uptime and performance guarantees for production systems

criteria.name: Multi-Tenancy Support
criteria.description: Built-in capabilities for tenant isolation and access control

criteria.name: OAuth Integration
criteria.description: Support for social logins and enterprise identity providers

criteria.name: Community Support
criteria.description: Availability of documentation, forums, and third-party tools

criteria.name: Customization Flexibility
criteria.description: Ability to modify authentication workflows and UI

verdict.summary: Clerk offers fastest initial implementation but creates vendor lock-in. Auth.js provides maximum flexibility with higher development overhead. Supabase Auth balances customization with database integration but requires more setup. Choose based on team expertise and long-term maintenance preferences.

use_cases.scenario: Startup building MVP with limited engineering resources
use_cases.reason: Rapid implementation with minimal infrastructure requirements

use_cases.scenario: Enterprise application requiring custom access control
use_cases.reason: Full control over authentication workflows and security policies
Seamless integration with PostgreSQL for multi-tenancy patterns

use_cases.scenario: Open-source project requiring no vendor dependencies
use_cases.reason: Leverages standard authentication protocols with no lock-in risk
